serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of king oyster mushroom vermicelli is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of sliced king oyster mushrooms and glass noodles with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central: Mushrooms, oyster, raw (FDC ID 169403) and Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), dry (FDC ID 169730) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for king oyster mushroom vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for oyster mushrooms (FDC ID 169403) and mung bean glass noodles (FDC ID 169730), weighted at approximately 50% mushroom and 50% cooked glass noodles by edible weight, with adjustment for cooking oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish) and soy/oyster sauce seasoning (~400 mg sodium per 100 g dish). This is a composite estimation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean glass noodles / cellophane noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for king oyster mushroom vermicelli as a composite dish. GI estimated at 35 based on the dominant glycemic contributor being mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles), which have a published GI of approximately 33–39 (low GI) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). King oyster mushrooms contribute negligible glycemic load. Composite dish GI estimated at 35 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 49.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 300 g serving provides approximately 1,260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean glass noodles and high mushroom fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: King oyster mushroom vermicelli has a low estimated GI of approximately 35 due to the mung bean glass noodle base; however, the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 17, so portion control remains important for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and mushroom beta-glucan content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (~0.4 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with king oyster mushrooms providing beta-glucans that may support LDL cholesterol reduction; the primary cardiovascular concern is the moderate sodium content from seasoning sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: King Oyster Mushroom Vermicelli is a Thai-influenced dish featuring sliced or shredded king oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus eryngii) stir-fried or braised with mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles / wun sen). The dish is seasoned with garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, and is typically garnished with spring onion. King oyster mushrooms have a firm, meaty texture with a mild umami flavour that absorbs the savoury sauce well, while the glass noodles provide a soft, slightly chewy, translucent texture. The dish is popular in Thai cuisine and is also widely served in Thai restaurants in Singapore, where it may appear on menus as a vegetarian-friendly option. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (~98 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides a useful source of dietary fibre, B vitamins (particularly niacin and riboflavin), and potassium from the mushrooms. The mung bean glass noodles contribute a low glycemic index (~35), making this dish relatively favourable for blood glucose management compared to wheat-based noodle dishes. The primary nutritional concern is the sodium content from soy and oyster sauces, which can be significant in restaurant preparations. King oyster mushrooms are a source of beta-glucans, which have been associated with immune modulation and modest LDL cholesterol reduction in clinical studies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for king oyster mushroom vermicelli was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for oyster mushrooms and mung bean glass noodles with cooking adjustments. GI is estimated from the analogous mung bean glass noodle GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for oyster mushrooms (FDC ID 169403) and mung bean cellophane noodles (FDC ID 169730), weighted approximately 50:50 by edible cooked weight with oil and sauce adjustments. A direct laboratory analysis of this dish would improve confidence to high. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this mushroom-noodle composite in the source database. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as king oyster mushrooms and mung bean noodles contain no trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol; naturally zero. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish; naturally zero. 9. GI estimated at 35 based on mung bean glass noodle GI from University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate). 10. The cuisine tag 'Seafood Dish' and 'Prawn' from the field survey source record are not reflected in this nutrient record as the dish as described is a mushroom-and-noodle preparation without seafood; if the restaurant version includes prawns, a separate record or variant note would be required. 11. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Thai dish, not a Singaporean dish; Thai is the mandatory local language per G6 multilingual rules.
